name: Kalenjin
description: Kalenjin is an umbrella term coined in the 1940s for a group of related Nilotic-speaking communities of the Rift Valley. The name comes from the phrase 'I say to you' (Kale a enjin), used in radio broadcasts to unite these communities politically. Sub-groups include the Nandi, Kipsigis, Tugen, Elgeyo, Marakwet, Pokot, Sabaot, and Terik. They are highland pastoralists and farmers known for their age-grade system, their role in Kenya's running dominance, and elaborate circumcision ceremonies.
cultural_highlights: Circumcision age-grade system — the central rite of passage that defines cohort identity and social obligation across all Kalenjin sub-groups
Tum — a traditional communication system using distinct horn calls to convey messages across long distances
Sigirion — ceremonial songs and poetry composed for specific age-grades, recited as civic duty
Koito — a traditional court dance performed at weddings, harvest festivals, and after successful cattle raids

food_traditions.name: Mursik
food_traditions.description: Fermented milk stored in a smoked gourd (sotet) — the defining Kalenjin beverage, a probiotic staple and gift of honour

food_traditions.name: Ugali with vegetables
food_traditions.description: Maize ugali with indigenous vegetables (rotich, saget) and fermented milk

food_traditions.name: Kimiet
food_traditions.description: Roasted or dried maize ground into a travel food — light, portable sustenance for warriors and herders

music_traditions: Sinendet — a thumb piano played during quiet evenings and storytelling
Leldet — competitive group singing with rhythmic stamping, performed at night dances
Arap — praise poetry performed solo by a man recounting his clan's history and achievements
